What is the problem this feature will solve?

As I am looking into ways to improve astropy performance, I can see that using the new'ish compiler feature of function multi-versioning should improve the performance of compiled C code, while retaining the portability of wheels. 

# Background
By default, GCC ensures that the compiled code can run on most hardware and only compiles for SSE2 on x86_64 systems (vast majority of the existing hardware that astronomers use). SSE2 was released in 2001, and since then, the CPUs have evolved significantly and provide a whole bunch of efficient (assembly) instructions. Of course, the highest performance could be extracted by writing specific functions for each targeted CPU but that would be insurmountable maintenance burden (even if we could take on converting the relevant functions). 

# Proposed solution
GCC (and Clang, but with caveats) provides an option to generate many different specialised functions when the targeted function is annotated with an `__attribute__` and a list of target architectures. 

For example, adding the following attribute and compiling with a recent gcc:
```c    
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
double sum_square(const double *restrict arr, const int64_t N) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    //This can also be vectorized if using -Ofast
    //or using #pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum) and compiling with -fopenmp-simd
    for(int64_t i=0;i<N;i++) sum += arr[i]*arr[i];

    return sum;
}
```
will generate the following:
- specialised `sum_square` functions for "default(==sse2), sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,skylake-cpus"
- a resolver function that checks the runtime CPU and calls the correct specialised function, i.e., no illegal instruction error at  runtime. See this [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for the generated assembly code (and potentially play around with compiler options and compiler version)

I am suggesting creating a new C macro that adds the FMV attributes or nothing depending on 

```c
#ifdef USE_FUNCTION_MULTI_VERSIONING
#define FMV(FUNC).     __attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,arch=skylake-avx512, ..."))) FUNC
#else
#define FMV(FUNC)      FUNC
#endif

//and then using:
FMV(static inline int adder( ...))
```


Requires >= GLIBC 2.23, according to [the docs](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-target_005fclones-function-attribute).

### Describe the desired outcome

Compiled C code within astropy uses the instructions available on the runtime CPU, rather than being restricted to instructions on 25 year-old hardware. 

### Additional context

I will note that function multi-versioning only improves the used instructions and does not necessarily enable vectorization. That would require additional steps; for example, for the above example of the `sum_squares` - since there is a summation and that answer can change due to numerical round-off from vectorization and different order of summing, the compiler will not generate vectorized code for the loop **unless** `-Ofast` is used, or `-fopenmp-simd` is used along with a `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)`. See the [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for more details (look for `vmulpd/vaddpd` instructions for vectorized code, and `vmulsd/vaddsd` instructions for unvectorized code on AVX+ instruction sets).

Now that I have read up on how to [create new ufuncs](https://numpy.org/devdocs/user/c-info.ufunc-tutorial.html) - those seem to be targeted more creating the same function for different input datatypes *rather* than utilising different hardware architecture (i.e., assembly instructions). 

The big benefit about my proposed solution of adding that single `target_clones` attribute is that the complexity should stay constant (and low) with time, and the maintenance burden would be only to add/drop hardware targets from that `target_clones` attribute. That attribute would be defined as a macro within one C header file (potentially autogenerated with python) and changing the definition would automatically change for all the annotated C functions.

Let me setup a benchmark with the astropy default and my proposed solution and I will report back. My first target will be the fast-sigma-clipping (which calls `compute_sigma_clipped_bounds`, and `wirth_median` both of which should benefit from the better instruction sets)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2342698512/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What is the problem this feature will solve?

As I am looking into ways to improve astropy performance, I can see that using the new'ish compiler feature of function multi-versioning should improve the performance of compiled C code, while retaining the portability of wheels. 

# Background
By default, GCC ensures that the compiled code can run on most hardware and only compiles for SSE2 on x86_64 systems (vast majority of the existing hardware that astronomers use). SSE2 was released in 2001, and since then, the CPUs have evolved significantly and provide a whole bunch of efficient (assembly) instructions. Of course, the highest performance could be extracted by writing specific functions for each targeted CPU but that would be insurmountable maintenance burden (even if we could take on converting the relevant functions). 

# Proposed solution
GCC (and Clang, but with caveats) provides an option to generate many different specialised functions when the targeted function is annotated with an `__attribute__` and a list of target architectures. 

For example, adding the following attribute and compiling with a recent gcc:
```c    
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
double sum_square(const double *restrict arr, const int64_t N) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    //This can also be vectorized if using -Ofast
    //or using #pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum) and compiling with -fopenmp-simd
    for(int64_t i=0;i<N;i++) sum += arr[i]*arr[i];

    return sum;
}
```
will generate the following:
- specialised `sum_square` functions for "default(==sse2), sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,skylake-cpus"
- a resolver function that checks the runtime CPU and calls the correct specialised function, i.e., no illegal instruction error at  runtime. See this [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for the generated assembly code (and potentially play around with compiler options and compiler version)

I am suggesting creating a new C macro that adds the FMV attributes or nothing depending on 

```c
#ifdef USE_FUNCTION_MULTI_VERSIONING
#define FMV(FUNC).     __attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,arch=skylake-avx512, ..."))) FUNC
#else
#define FMV(FUNC)      FUNC
#endif

//and then using:
FMV(static inline int adder( ...))
```


Requires >= GLIBC 2.23, according to [the docs](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-target_005fclones-function-attribute).

### Describe the desired outcome

Compiled C code within astropy uses the instructions available on the runtime CPU, rather than being restricted to instructions on 25 year-old hardware. 

### Additional context

I will note that function multi-versioning only improves the used instructions and does not necessarily enable vectorization. That would require additional steps; for example, for the above example of the `sum_squares` - since there is a summation and that answer can change due to numerical round-off from vectorization and different order of summing, the compiler will not generate vectorized code for the loop **unless** `-Ofast` is used, or `-fopenmp-simd` is used along with a `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)`. See the [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for more details (look for `vmulpd/vaddpd` instructions for vectorized code, and `vmulsd/vaddsd` instructions for unvectorized code on AVX+ instruction sets).

For example, gcc10.1 on x86_64 generates separate functions for each of specified targets for this [the godbolt example](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM). So gcc10 on manylinux2014 should be fine to compile and run. 



^The only constraint I could see was that the requirement for GLIBC >= 2.23 on powerpc. Here's the complete text under `target_clones` from the [function attributes page](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html)

```
target_clones (options)

    The target_clones attribute is used to specify that a function be cloned into multiple versions compiled with different target options than specified on the command line. The supported options and restrictions are the same as for target attribute.

    For instance, on an x86, you could compile a function with target_clones("sse4.1,avx"). GCC creates two function clones, one compiled with -msse4.1 and another with -mavx.

    On a PowerPC, you can compile a function with target_clones("cpu=power9,default"). GCC will create two function clones, one compiled with -mcpu=power9 and another with the default options. GCC must be configured to use GLIBC 2.23 or newer in order to use the target_clones attribute.

    It also creates a resolver function (see the ifunc attribute above) that dynamically selects a clone suitable for current architecture. The resolver is created only if there is a usage of a function with target_clones attribute.

    Note that any subsequent call of a function without target_clone from a target_clone caller will not lead to copying (target clone) of the called function. If you want to enforce such behaviour, we recommend declaring the calling function with the flatten attribute?
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2343588745/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The only drawback is that the massive complexity is justified because performance is a crucial goal for numpy. I am not sure that that is the case for astropy (and it's not lost on me that I am saying this as a performance-obsessed person) - both the implementation effort now and the future maintenance burden will be pretty high if we adopt the entire machinery for numpy simd source generation, compilation, and runtime dispatch as well as the packaging system. 

> I found one by @\seiko2plus called https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23153, which removed lots of lines with things like #define NPY_GCC_TARGET_AVX512_SKX __attribute__((target("avx512f,avx512dq,avx512vl,avx512bw,avx512cd"))). If numpy moved away from it after using it for years, why is this the right solution for us?

Because numpy has multiple dispatch for dedicated simd functions, having the target attributes associated with the functions didn't make sense. (Though, in my experience, the `target` attribute did not work for multiple architectures and the expectation for function multi-versioning was to write dedicated functions for *each* target. Thankfully, `target_clones` automatically creates those functions in assembly for each target arch and the resolver function) 

> Also, on maintainer burden, while perhaps on the direct implementation side all we are committing to is having to update compiler attributes -- which frankly I already doubt the astropy maintainers are really the right crowd for -- a different maintainer burden is bugs associated with acceleration. 

Hmm - I am probably not being clear in what I mean. A change in the typical *function* attributes could be something like this:

```c
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
```
to something like:

```c
__attribute__((target_clones("default,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512,arch=sapphirerapids")))
```
i.e., drop `SSE4.2` targets and add the target for the latest Intel Sapphire Rapids CPU, and that change would be in one line within a header file. That seems relatively straightforward to me, assuming that there has been user request for building astropy specifically for some new fancy supercomputer.

> Numpy has a quite a few of these (including annoying things like different rounding errors), and they are hard to diagnose. Hence, for diagnosis one really should have a mechanism to let a bug reporter tell which acceleration path was actually taken. Numpy has one (https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/24420), but how are we going to do that? Also, given that such bugs will occur, we have to have a mechanism that allows users to opt out of acceleration. What is that going to be? If we follow numpy procedures, we get that all for free.

Totally agree - with the auto-generated resolver function, there is no way for the user to specify if they would like to run, 
say the `SSE` function on an `AVX512` machine. 

The vectorisation round-off errors are more nuanced - which is one of the reasons why compilers do not auto-vectorise code *unless* `-Ofast` is used. There is always scope for a numerical mismatch with extended precision that a fused-multipy-add (FMA) offers compared to separate multiply and adds. FMA wins in my perspective - using FMA operations gives *both* higher throughput and better accuracy (but leads to numerical discrepancies with scalar code)

To be clear - I am 100% with you - if astropy wants to properly improve performance within the C codes, then adopting the numpy machinery seems like a no-brainer to me. I would **love** to incorporate that numpy machinery into astropy, but such a project might be better off as a dedicated and collaborative effort (possibly requiring an APE?). For instance, I couldn't read, understand, and port over the SIMD source generation, compilation, and distribution machinery into astropy within the remaining hours of the funded project.

What is the problem this feature will solve?

As I am looking into ways to improve astropy performance, I can see that using the new'ish compiler feature of function multi-versioning should improve the performance of compiled C code, while retaining the portability of wheels. 

# Background
By default, GCC ensures that the compiled code can run on most hardware and only compiles for SSE2 on x86_64 systems (vast majority of the existing hardware that astronomers use). SSE2 was released in 2001, and since then, the CPUs have evolved significantly and provide a whole bunch of efficient (assembly) instructions. Of course, the highest performance could be extracted by writing specific functions for each targeted CPU but that would be insurmountable maintenance burden (even if we could take on converting the relevant functions). 

# Proposed solution
GCC (and Clang, but with caveats) provides an option to generate many different specialised functions when the targeted function is annotated with an `__attribute__` and a list of target architectures. 

For example, adding the following attribute and compiling with a recent gcc:
```c    
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
double sum_square(const double *restrict arr, const int64_t N) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    //This can also be vectorized if using -Ofast
    //or using #pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum) and compiling with -fopenmp-simd
    for(int64_t i=0;i<N;i++) sum += arr[i]*arr[i];

    return sum;
}
```
will generate the following:
- specialised `sum_square` functions for "default(==sse2), sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,skylake-cpus"
- a resolver function that checks the runtime CPU and calls the correct specialised function, i.e., no illegal instruction error at  runtime. See this [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for the generated assembly code (and potentially play around with compiler options and compiler version)

I am suggesting creating a new C macro that adds the FMV attributes or nothing depending on 

```c
#ifdef USE_FUNCTION_MULTI_VERSIONING
#define FMV(FUNC).     __attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,arch=skylake-avx512, ..."))) FUNC
#else
#define FMV(FUNC)      FUNC
#endif

//and then using:
FMV(static inline int adder( ...))
```


Requires >= GLIBC 2.23, according to [the docs](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-target_005fclones-function-attribute).

### Describe the desired outcome

Compiled C code within astropy uses the instructions available on the runtime CPU, rather than being restricted to instructions on 25 year-old hardware. 

### Additional context

I will note that function multi-versioning only improves the used instructions and does not necessarily enable vectorization. That would require additional steps; for example, for the above example of the `sum_squares` - since there is a summation and that answer can change due to numerical round-off from vectorization and different order of summing, the compiler will not generate vectorized code for the loop **unless** `-Ofast` is used, or `-fopenmp-simd` is used along with a `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)`. See the [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for more details (look for `vmulpd/vaddpd` instructions for vectorized code, and `vmulsd/vaddsd` instructions for unvectorized code on AVX+ instruction sets).

These are the bits that could use extra info/helpful links:

1. Tax implications for filling out the contractor form with NumFOCUS - there are a few options to consider here (at least these):
  - US citizen/green card holder living in USA
  - Non-US citizens living in USA (may be visa status also impacts here, e.g., may be international students are not eligible to work extra?)
  - US citizen/green card holder living abroad 
  - Non-USA nationals living outside of USA

While everyone still needs to do their due diligence and ideally consult with a tax professional in their countries to understand tax implications, having some helpful web links or various scenarios would be handy. Hopefully, NumFOCUS has some material available already. 

2. Break down the required steps to getting paid after project is funded:
  - Sign contract with NumFOCUS
  - Create personal account and possibly business account with OpenCollective
  - (Do the work)
  - Submit (monthly/quarterly) invoices to OpenCollective. This is two-step - once for the "text" invoice on OpenCollective but it requires an itemized pdf invoice as well. PDF invoice needs to contain the following:
    -  hours worked
    - description of the work performed
    - hourly rate
    - billing date range
    - Invoice Number
 
The expected granularity of the work description is unclear - are we expected to provide details for each chunk of work? For example, would each chunk of work need to have a separate work description and time log - e.g., "profile histogram code with astropy (1 hr)", or does a broad work description suffice - e.g., "performance optimisation (10 hrs)". 

A separate thing that could be useful in the proposal phase would be different rubrics for time estimates based on the astropy package experience. As a software expert but an *astropy newbie*, I am surprised by how much time I have spent reading the infrastructure (e.g., CI jobs), documentation, and communication over GitHub / Slack.

Hopefully, this is helpful! My goal here is to see if we can improve the future contributor/developer experience - I am not fussed about myself.

What is the problem this feature will solve?

As I am looking into ways to improve astropy performance, I can see that using the new'ish compiler feature of function multi-versioning should improve the performance of compiled C code, while retaining the portability of wheels. 

# Background
By default, GCC ensures that the compiled code can run on most hardware and only compiles for SSE2 on x86_64 systems (vast majority of the existing hardware that astronomers use). SSE2 was released in 2001, and since then, the CPUs have evolved significantly and provide a whole bunch of efficient (assembly) instructions. Of course, the highest performance could be extracted by writing specific functions for each targeted CPU but that would be insurmountable maintenance burden (even if we could take on converting the relevant functions). 

# Proposed solution
GCC (and Clang, but with caveats) provides an option to generate many different specialised functions when the targeted function is annotated with an `__attribute__` and a list of target architectures. 

For example, adding the following attribute and compiling with a recent gcc:
```c    
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
double sum_square(const double *restrict arr, const int64_t N) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    //This can also be vectorized if using -Ofast
    //or using #pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum) and compiling with -fopenmp-simd
    for(int64_t i=0;i<N;i++) sum += arr[i]*arr[i];

    return sum;
}
```
will generate the following:
- specialised `sum_square` functions for "default(==sse2), sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,skylake-cpus"
- a resolver function that checks the runtime CPU and calls the correct specialised function, i.e., no illegal instruction error at  runtime. See this [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for the generated assembly code (and potentially play around with compiler options and compiler version)

I am suggesting creating a new C macro that adds the FMV attributes or nothing depending on 

```c
#ifdef USE_FUNCTION_MULTI_VERSIONING
#define FMV(FUNC).     __attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,arch=skylake-avx512, ..."))) FUNC
#else
#define FMV(FUNC)      FUNC
#endif

//and then using:
FMV(static inline int adder( ...))
```


Requires >= GLIBC 2.23, according to [the docs](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-target_005fclones-function-attribute).

### Describe the desired outcome

Compiled C code within astropy uses the instructions available on the runtime CPU, rather than being restricted to instructions on 25 year-old hardware. 

### Additional context

I will note that function multi-versioning only improves the used instructions and does not necessarily enable vectorization. That would require additional steps; for example, for the above example of the `sum_squares` - since there is a summation and that answer can change due to numerical round-off from vectorization and different order of summing, the compiler will not generate vectorized code for the loop **unless** `-Ofast` is used, or `-fopenmp-simd` is used along with a `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)`. See the [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for more details (look for `vmulpd/vaddpd` instructions for vectorized code, and `vmulsd/vaddsd` instructions for unvectorized code on AVX+ instruction sets).

Given that astropy is a popular software and installed on a variety of OS + ISAs, and with users that (likely) have a broad range of use-cases, implementing native intrinsics on certain hardware (I know the `x86_64` kinds primarily) while leaving the remaining ISAs as default, does not feel like a good solution :(

The Highway example you linked on the [numpy issue](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/21057) look good, but the example case is simple, and it still requires fairly extensive modification of the source. For example, [this godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/e6fhqPbdT) shows my attempt at the same function, with added function attributes of `-Ofast` and `target_clones(relevant ISA list)`. gcc14.2 generates packed instructions along with the necessary CPU feature detection + function dispatch (but does require compiling with `-mprefer-vector-width=512` to get the `zmm` instructions for AVX512+). 

The biggest advantage of the `target_clones` is that the compiler does the heavy lifting as long as sufficient compiler hints are at the function level. This extra annotation is fixed and *independent* of the actual code within the function body, reducing the maintenance burden. These compiler hints could also be wrapped up in a generic macro that expands to the different ISA flavours for a given OS + ARCH. The biggest disadvantage is that there is no user-level control over  (or even tracking the) chosen function at runtime. 

To be clear, I am trying to understand the tradeoffs here, including creating an additional external package dependency and adding C++ code (currently, there isn't any within astropy). Secondarily, if feasible, my goal would be to create a solution that works for all astropy users. May the benchmarks with the `target_clones` would be a good indicator of whether or not to pursue this `target_clones` approach or adopt an external library (and potentially C++) to get performance boosts!

Thank you again for your insights - both here and on the numpy issue!

+# Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst
+
+import os
+from numpy import get_include as get_numpy_include
+from distutils.extension import Extension
+from astropy_helpers.openmp_helpers import add_openmp_flags_if_available
+
+C_CONVOLVE_PKGDIR = os.path.relpath(os.path.dirname(__file__))
+
+SRC_FILES = [os.path.join(C_CONVOLVE_PKGDIR, filename)
+              for filename in ['boundary_none_direct.c',
+                               'boundary_padded_direct.c']]
+
+def get_extensions():
+    # Add '-Rpass-missed=.*' to ``extra_compile_args`` when compiling with clang
+    # to report missed optimizations
+    c_convolve_ext = Extension(name='c_convolve', sources=SRC_FILES,
+                 extra_compile_args=['-O3', '-fPIC'],","path":"astropy/convolution/setup_package.py","commit_id":"c1c6a709ccd0e640180b534d1e6c62595c4f17ca","original_commit_id":"1722fb4c81c70a1e535c0d903fa5ba5a605a933f","user":{"login":"manodeep","id":3004941,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMDQ5NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3004941?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep","html_url":"https://github.com/manodeep","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Though this is a very old issue, since I came across this while looking through performance->compiler flags->openmp issues, I will note that it used to be the case that `-O3` was `-O2 -ftree-vectorize` but that seems to be no longer the case. convolution.convolve","user":{"login":"jamienoss","id":5013975,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwMTM5NzU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5013975?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss","html_url":"https://github.com/jamienoss","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"- [Convolution investigation and benchmark](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/1)
- [Performance Notes](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/2)

 ## Perf. changes

- Separate code for interpolating NaN values and when not. __Major perf. boost.__
- If ``nan_treatment=‘interpolate’``, pre-check image for presence of NaN values,
  if none exist, don’t call NaN interpolation code. __Major perf. boost.__
- Hoist loop dependent variable computations to correct loop scope.
- Untangle NaN interpolation and kernel normalization. Don’t normalize kernel until
  after convolution instead of doing so before and again after if not requested.
- Thread with OPENMP. Required replacing Cython code (.pyx) with raw C. __Major perf. boost.__
- For ``boundary in [‘fill’, ‘extend’, ‘wrap’]`` use a padded array instead of specific index logic. __Major perf. boost.__
  This has a significant performance boost, though it does increase the memory footprint.
  It also signifcantly reduces the code base.
- Improve DASK performance as previous Cython code used Python division and thus
  meant reacquiring the GIL per loop iteration.
  _Note:_ this could have been solved by adding the decorator: ``@cython.cdivision(True)`` to the Cython functions.
  _Note:_ Using ``convolve`` threaded with DASK is still more performant than before however,
  this will overcommit threads and could temporarily make the machine unresponsive. When using DASK
  it is advised to make ``n_threads=1``, and can still improve runtime perf. by 2 compared to previous implementation.
  [github issue](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/5)
- Hoist all exceptions to as early as possible, especially before memory allocation or heavy(ish) computations.

 ## Non Perf. changes

- Correct doc string for default boundary case ``None`` -> ``’fill’``.

Signed-off-by: James Noss <jnoss@stsci.edu>

![pr-bench-figure](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5013975/37730059-0d5c4c8c-2d15-11e8-9ad1-f88a4e7fd897.png)

enhancements for convolution.convolve","user":{"login":"jamienoss","id":5013975,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwMTM5NzU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5013975?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss","html_url":"https://github.com/jamienoss","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jamienoss/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"- [Convolution investigation and benchmark](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/1)
- [Performance Notes](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/2)

 ## Perf. changes

- Separate code for interpolating NaN values and when not. __Major perf. boost.__
- If ``nan_treatment=‘interpolate’``, pre-check image for presence of NaN values,
  if none exist, don’t call NaN interpolation code. __Major perf. boost.__
- Hoist loop dependent variable computations to correct loop scope.
- Untangle NaN interpolation and kernel normalization. Don’t normalize kernel until
  after convolution instead of doing so before and again after if not requested.
- Thread with OPENMP. Required replacing Cython code (.pyx) with raw C. __Major perf. boost.__
- For ``boundary in [‘fill’, ‘extend’, ‘wrap’]`` use a padded array instead of specific index logic. __Major perf. boost.__
  This has a significant performance boost, though it does increase the memory footprint.
  It also signifcantly reduces the code base.
- Improve DASK performance as previous Cython code used Python division and thus
  meant reacquiring the GIL per loop iteration.
  _Note:_ this could have been solved by adding the decorator: ``@cython.cdivision(True)`` to the Cython functions.
  _Note:_ Using ``convolve`` threaded with DASK is still more performant than before however,
  this will overcommit threads and could temporarily make the machine unresponsive. When using DASK
  it is advised to make ``n_threads=1``, and can still improve runtime perf. by 2 compared to previous implementation.
  [github issue](https://github.com/jamienoss/astropy/issues/5)
- Hoist all exceptions to as early as possible, especially before memory allocation or heavy(ish) computations.

 ## Non Perf. changes

- Correct doc string for default boundary case ``None`` -> ``’fill’``.

Signed-off-by: James Noss <jnoss@stsci.edu>

![pr-bench-figure](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5013975/37730059-0d5c4c8c-2d15-11e8-9ad1-f88a4e7fd897.png)

3.13","color":"f7c6c7","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-06-20T15:42:04Z","updated_at":"2024-08-21T04:35:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This issue is to plan and keep track on the effort to add support for CPython 3.13 .
See [PEP 719](https://peps.python.org/pep-0719/) for the release schedule.
At the time of opening, version `3.13.0b2` is available, and NumPy is already publishing nightly wheels for it.

Here's the chronological task list to complete before we can declare support for it.
- [x] pass all tests (#16659)
- [x] #16600 
- [x] #16596 
- [ ] publish `cp313` wheels to PyPI: 
  - [x] CPython 3.13.0rc1 needs to be released https://pythoninsider.blogspot.com/2024/08/python-3130-release-candidate-1-released.html?m=1
  - [x] update manylinux
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/pull/1652
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/pull/1660
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/actions/runs/10226520340
  - [x] update cibuildwheel
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/pull/1949
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/pull/1950
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/releases/tag/v2.20.0
  - [x] https://github.com/OpenAstronomy/github-actions-workflows/pull/220
  - [ ] cut a release or add cp313 wheels to the current latest release (the latter is more cumbersome, see https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/discussions/888)
- [x] https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16848
- [ ] #16858 
- [ ] If we want Python 3.13 support in v6.1.x series, we also need to apply #16858 and relevant compatibility fixes to v6.1.x branch.

I'll also list which of our core dependencies already have compatible and stable wheels on PyPI, but note that it isn't a necessary condition to make our own.
 - [x] pyerfa (abi3)
 - [x] numpy v2.1.0 (2024-08-18)
 - [x] PyYAML https://github.com/yaml/pyyaml/issues/818

Supporting the experimental free-threading (a.k.a "nogil") build is a non-goal for this issue (although I note that it's currently blocked by https://github.com/liberfa/pyerfa/issues/152)

Effectively add the function `PyUnstable_Module_SetGIL()` to enable GIL on free-threaded python (though GIL enabled seems to be set as the [default](https://docs.python.org/3.13/c-api/module.html#c.Py_mod_gil))

```c
PyMODINIT_FUNC
PyInit__module(void)
{
    PyObject *mod = PyModule_Create(&module);
    if (mod == NULL) {
        return NULL;
    }

#ifdef Py_GIL_DISABLED
    PyUnstable_Module_SetGIL(mod, Py_MOD_GIL_USED); 
#endif
}
```

At the time of opening, version `3.13.0b2` is available, and NumPy is already publishing nightly wheels for it.

Here's the chronological task list to complete before we can declare support for it.
- [x] pass all tests (#16659)
- [x] #16600 
- [x] #16596 
- [ ] publish `cp313` wheels to PyPI: 
  - [x] CPython 3.13.0rc1 needs to be released https://pythoninsider.blogspot.com/2024/08/python-3130-release-candidate-1-released.html?m=1
  - [x] update manylinux
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/pull/1652
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/pull/1660
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/actions/runs/10226520340

    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/pull/1949
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/pull/1950
    - [x] https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/releases/tag/v2.20.0

What is the problem this feature will solve?

As I am looking into ways to improve astropy performance, I can see that using the new'ish compiler feature of function multi-versioning should improve the performance of compiled C code, while retaining the portability of wheels. 

# Background
By default, GCC ensures that the compiled code can run on most hardware and only compiles for SSE2 on x86_64 systems (vast majority of the existing hardware that astronomers use). SSE2 was released in 2001, and since then, the CPUs have evolved significantly and provide a whole bunch of efficient (assembly) instructions. Of course, the highest performance could be extracted by writing specific functions for each targeted CPU but that would be insurmountable maintenance burden (even if we could take on converting the relevant functions). 

# Proposed solution
GCC (and Clang, but with caveats) provides an option to generate many different specialised functions when the targeted function is annotated with an `__attribute__` and a list of target architectures. 

For example, adding the following attribute and compiling with a recent gcc:
```c    
__attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,arch=skylake-avx512")))
double sum_square(const double *restrict arr, const int64_t N) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    //This can also be vectorized if using -Ofast
    //or using #pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum) and compiling with -fopenmp-simd
    for(int64_t i=0;i<N;i++) sum += arr[i]*arr[i];

    return sum;
}
```
will generate the following:
- specialised `sum_square` functions for "default(==sse2), sse4.2,avx,avx2,avx512f,skylake-cpus"
- a resolver function that checks the runtime CPU and calls the correct specialised function, i.e., no illegal instruction error at  runtime. See this [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for the generated assembly code (and potentially play around with compiler options and compiler version)

I am suggesting creating a new C macro that adds the FMV attributes or nothing depending on 

```c
#ifdef USE_FUNCTION_MULTI_VERSIONING
#define FMV(FUNC).     __attribute__((target_clones("default,sse4.2,avx,avx2,arch=skylake-avx512, ..."))) FUNC
#else
#define FMV(FUNC)      FUNC
#endif

//and then using:
FMV(static inline int adder( ...))
```


Requires >= GLIBC 2.23, according to [the docs](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-target_005fclones-function-attribute).

### Describe the desired outcome

Compiled C code within astropy uses the instructions available on the runtime CPU, rather than being restricted to instructions on 25 year-old hardware. 

### Additional context

I will note that function multi-versioning only improves the used instructions and does not necessarily enable vectorization. That would require additional steps; for example, for the above example of the `sum_squares` - since there is a summation and that answer can change due to numerical round-off from vectorization and different order of summing, the compiler will not generate vectorized code for the loop **unless** `-Ofast` is used, or `-fopenmp-simd` is used along with a `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)`. See the [godbolt link](https://godbolt.org/z/bE44Ej5PM) for more details (look for `vmulpd/vaddpd` instructions for vectorized code, and `vmulsd/vaddsd` instructions for unvectorized code on AVX+ instruction sets).

Are you targeting specific sub-package(s) with this? `wcs`?

@pllim I was thinking of `astropy.stats` first but other potential options are `astropy.convolution` and `astropy.timeseries` (based on [this search](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Aastropy%2Fastropy++language%3AC+NOT+path%3A%2F%5Ecextern%5C%2F%2F+NOT+path%3A%2F%5Eastropy%5C%2Fwcs%5C%2F%2F+NOT+path%3A%2F%5Eastropy%5C%2Fio%5C%2Ffits%5C%2F%2F&type=code)). I was going to avoid starting with cextern packages - i.e., skip wcs, cfitsio at least for now. Those changes would be better off being integrated into the upstream packages - I suspect wcs could particularly benefit from vectorization. 

> Intriguing - since quite a number of our C routines use numpy ufuncs, we probably can use some of the numpy machinery. Indeed, hopefully we can re-use their stuff more generally.

@mhvk I looked into numpy simd machinery over the past two days and that is a complex beast. What would be a good starting point for investigating numpy ufuncs (to hook into into numpy down the line)?

pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep calling wcsset if the wcsprm is unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

modifying wcsprm in-place to prevent multi-threading issues","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":188323,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxODgzMjM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/wcs","name":"wcs","color":"c7def8","default":false,"description":null},{"id":1109127675,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTA5MTI3Njc1","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/needs-discussion","name":"needs-discussion","color":"621291","default":false,"description":""},{"id":6921521858,"node_id":"LA_kwDOAB_CCc8AAAABnI4Kwg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/multi-threading","name":"multi-threading","color":"CCD2E9","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-07T21:59:17Z","updated_at":"2024-05-07T23:33:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is a different issue from:

* https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16244
* https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245

and covers a potential issue in the astropy WCSLIB wrappers which could in theory cause multi-threading problems, though I don't have a specific example that will trigger this at this point.

In ``PyWcsprm``, we store undefined values as NaN - however just before/after we call any WCSLIB function, we convert **in-place** these NaN values to ``UNDEFINED`` (a specific floating-point value) and then back afterwards. using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python``. This in-place modification isn't thread-safe, and it would be nice to find another way to do it.

Unfortunately we can't do the conversion in the getters/setters, at least not for ones returning arrays/vectors, since users might do e.g.:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

that is, getting the array and modifying it in-place without invoking the setter.

One possible solution would be that when we convert between NaN and undefined, we actually create temporary copies of the WCS and modify those rather than doing any in-place modification. This will double memory usage and might introduce a very small performance penalty, but both would probably have minimal impact on users and be safer. So in practice ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` would return modified copies and not operate in-place.

There might be other/better solutions though, so this issue is meant to be for discussion at this stage.

This is unlike the code within [`wcs_types()`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/cextern/wcslib/C/wcs.c#L2913-L2914) where `malloc/calloc` is invoked and that changes the *value* of the variable (i..e, a new address)

/nstarman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":86,"closed_issues":40,"state":"open","created_at":"2023-10-14T22:16:29Z","updated_at":"2024-05-07T22:19:35Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-05-07T22:18:02Z","updated_at":"2024-05-07T23:53:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/pulls/16411","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411","diff_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

This fix also ensures that the solution continues to work in the future when a new field might be introduced where changed values require a `wcsset` call.

(The correct solution would require modifying *all* relevant getters (where the list of relevant getters might change in the future) to store the present values within a malloc'ed buffer, the number of data bytes, and another address that contains the memory address the getter refers to. Any call to `wcsset` would then have to compare the previously stored values with the current contents,  potentially as a `memcmp` assuming no padding bytes are involved (i.e., structs containing padding bytes between fields) are involved. Such a correct fix would be more complex and more invasive than Tom's fix)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2099491326/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

issues","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":188323,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxODgzMjM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/wcs","name":"wcs","color":"c7def8","default":false,"description":null},{"id":1109127675,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTA5MTI3Njc1","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/needs-discussion","name":"needs-discussion","color":"621291","default":false,"description":""},{"id":6921521858,"node_id":"LA_kwDOAB_CCc8AAAABnI4Kwg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/multi-threading","name":"multi-threading","color":"CCD2E9","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-05-07T21:59:17Z","updated_at":"2024-05-07T23:59:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is a different issue from:

* https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16244
* https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245

and covers a potential issue in the astropy WCSLIB wrappers which could in theory cause multi-threading problems, though I don't have a specific example that will trigger this at this point.

In ``PyWcsprm``, we store undefined values as NaN - however just before/after we call any WCSLIB function, we convert **in-place** these NaN values to ``UNDEFINED`` (a specific floating-point value) and then back afterwards. using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python``. This in-place modification isn't thread-safe, and it would be nice to find another way to do it.

Unfortunately we can't do the conversion in the getters/setters, at least not for ones returning arrays/vectors, since users might do e.g.:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

that is, getting the array and modifying it in-place without invoking the setter.

One possible solution would be that when we convert between NaN and undefined, we actually create temporary copies of the WCS and modify those rather than doing any in-place modification. This will double memory usage and might introduce a very small performance penalty, but both would probably have minimal impact on users and be safer. So in practice ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` would return modified copies and not operate in-place.

There might be other/better solutions though, so this issue is meant to be for discussion at this stage.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26409","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26409/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26409/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26409/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26409","id":2288829256,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86IbMNI","number":26409,"title":"DOC: Add clarification about returned indices for `np.searchsorted` with `sorter`","user":{"login":"manodeep","id":3004941,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMDQ5NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3004941?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep","html_url":"https://github.com/manodeep","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/manodeep/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359990,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5OTkw","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/04%20-%20Documentation","name":"04 - Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-05-10T03:10:03Z","updated_at":"2024-05-10T03:10:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation about the returned indices from `np.searchsorted` could be improved by clarifying that the returned indices refer to the `sorter` array and not the original (unsorted) array. 

Now that I have manually confirmed that the indices do refer to `sorter`, I can also see that the [`searchsorted` documentation](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.searchsorted.html) contains this line - *Find the indices into a sorted array a such that* - but I failed to make that connection despite reading the documentation multiple times. 

By adding something like "when `sorter` is present, then the returned indices refer to the sorter array (i.e., `a[sorter[indices]] == v`" (or equivalently, an example that shows the use of `sorter` and how the returned indices match the queried values). Even a pretend test code like the following would demonstrate the semantics for the return value for `np.searchsorted` when used with `sorter`.

```python
from numpy.random import default_rng

rng = default_rng()
a_size = 30
a_max = 100
a = rng.choice(a_max, size=a_size, replace=False)
sorter = np.argsort(a)

find_size = 5
find_vals = np.random.choice(a, size=find_size)

idx = np.searchsorted(a, find_vals, sorter=sorter)

np.testing.assert_array_equal(find_vals, a[sorter[idx]])
```

(FIrst time opening an issue - apologies in advance if I am not correctly following some process)

### Description

From WCSLIB release notes:
```
* C library

  - Until now, wcsset() always operated unconditionally - the wcsprm
    struct was set or reset regardless of its current state.  Likewise
    the various *set() functions for the other structs.

    However, in some situations, particularly in threaded applications,
    it is desirable to have wcsset(), etc., check the state of the
    struct and return immediately if it has already been set.  This may
    now be accomplished by setting wcsprm::flag == 1 (instead of 0)
    before calling wcsset().  Likewise for the other structs.  This sets
    a "bypass" flag within the struct itself.

  - A new function, wcsenq(), queries the state of the wcsprm struct,
    specifically whether WCSLIB is managing its memory, whether the
    struct has been set, and whether or not it is in bypass mode, and
    whether it is self-consistent.  There are corresponding functions
    for the other structs: celenq(), disenq(), linenq(), prjenq(),
    spcenq(), and tabenq().  Please refer to the WCSLIB manual.

  - In the C test suite, modified twcs to test wcsenq() and also
    wcsset() in bypass mode.

  - Quelled nuisance compiler warnings in wcsbth(), wcspih(), wcsp2s(),
    and wcshdo().
```

Related to https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245

Of special interest to @astrofrog @manodeep

Setting `wcs->flag=0` or any other value will execute the thread-race condition. 

On a different note, I am seeing an odd compile failure on Windows [here](https://github.com/manodeep/wcslib-build/actions/runs/9079178270/job/24947869550)

```sh
Run make WCSLIB_VERSION=8.3
gcc -Wall -Wextra -O2 -fopenmp -Iwcslib-8.3/C -Iwcslib-8.3 -fsanitize=undefined -fsanitize=bounds -fsanitize=address -fsanitize-undefined-trap-on-error -fstack-protector-all test_wcs_threads.c wcslib-8.3/C/libwcs-8.3.a -lm -fopenmp -o test_wcs_threads
In file included from wcslib-8.3/C/wcslib.h:49,
                 from test_wcs_threads.c:8:
wcslib-8.3/C/wcs.h:2301:5: error: conflicting types for 'wcsset'; have 'int(struct wcsprm *)'
 2301 | int wcsset(struct wcsprm *wcs);
      |     ^~~~~~
In file included from test_wcs_threads.c:5:
D:/a/_temp/msys64/mingw64/include/string.h:183:20: note: previous declaration of 'wcsset' with type 'wchar_t *(wchar_t *, wchar_t)' {aka 'short unsigned int *(short unsigned int *, short unsigned int)'}
  183 |   wchar_t *__cdecl wcsset(wchar_t *_Str,wchar_t _Val) __MINGW_ATTRIB_DEPRECATED_MSVC2005;
      |                    ^~~~~~
make: *** [Makefile:14: test_wcs_threads] Error 1
Error: Process completed with exit code 2.
```
Apparently, `string.h` on Windows contains a function called `wcsset`, and that is throwing the compiler off. This presumably occurred before too and has been solved within astropy.

/nstarman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":82,"closed_issues":53,"state":"open","created_at":"2023-10-14T22:16:29Z","updated_at":"2024-05-14T12:40:12Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-05-07T22:18:02Z","updated_at":"2024-05-14T12:47:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/pulls/16411","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411","diff_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16411.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

This can (hopefully) detect cases where some values of an array/list parameter (i.e., where the getters are invoked, rather than the setters) have been changed and that `wcsset()` needs to be called again.

calling wcsset if the wcsprm is unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep calling wcsset if the wcsprm is unchanged","user":{"login":"astrofrog","id":314716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNDcxNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/314716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog","html_url":"https://github.com/astrofrog","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrofrog/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request is an attempt to fix https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 - it is just a proof of concept at this point and should not be merged yet, we first need to agree whether this is a reasonable workaround. The issues in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16245 is caused fundamentally by ``wcsset`` not being thread-safe and being called from different threads. However, these calls are not necessary because the WCS is not changing between all the calls. So the approach in this PR is to keep track of what the ``wcsprm`` was after the last time ``wcsset`` was called, and exit early if it hasn't changed since.

Note that using ``flag`` as set by ``note_change`` is not sufficient, because users can modify some of the array attributes of the WCS in-place, such as:

```python
wcs.wcs.cdelt[1] = 1
```

which won't trigger ``note_change``.

As also noted in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16409, using ``wcsprm_python2c`` and ``wcsprm_c2python`` itself is not really thread-safe though that can be addressed separately as it is used in other places too and luckily doesn't seem to actually trigger threading issues in practice. With this PR, the example in #16245 outputs:

```
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
Mismatching elements: 0
```

I need to assess what the performance impact of this PR is.

cc @manodeep
